mnemonics.title: UDP vs TCP Quick Recall
mnemonics.content: UDP: Unreliable, Delivery Not Guaranteed / TCP: Transmission Control Protocol - Reliable, Ordered, Error-Checked. Remember: 'U Don't Promise' (UDP) vs 'Take Care Please' (TCP)

mnemonics.title: TCP Three-Way Handshake
mnemonics.content: SYN → SYN-ACK → ACK. Mnemonic: 'SYN, SYN-ACK, ACK - Let's Establish Contact' or 'Sam Yells: Start Your Acknowledgment Now'

mnemonics.title: TCP Flags Order
mnemonics.content: URG-ACK-PSH-RST-SYN-FIN. Remember: 'U A P R S F' = 'Urgently Ask People To Rest, Sleep, Finish' or 'U A Proud Soldier Ready For Battle'

mnemonics.title: Flow Control Mechanism
mnemonics.content: Sliding Window uses 'Send Window' and 'Receive Window'. Mnemonic: 'SWRW' = 'Sending Windows Regulate Flow' or remember: 'Window Size = Receiver's Buffer Space'

mnemonics.title: TCP States
mnemonics.content: LISTEN → SYN-SENT → SYN-RECEIVED → ESTABLISHED → FIN-WAIT → CLOSE-WAIT → LAST-ACK → CLOSED. Mnemonic: 'Little Soldiers Fight Courageously, Enduring Great Battles, Finally Coming Home' (Listen-Syn-Fin-Established-Wait-Close-Last-Closed)

acronyms.usage: Used for fast, connectionless, unreliable数据传输 like video streaming, DNS, VoIP where speed matters more than reliability
Used for reliable, ordered, error-checked communication like web browsing, email, file transfer where data integrity is crucial
Handles addressing and routing packets across networks at Layer 3 (Network Layer)
First step in TCP 3-way handshake to initiate a connection; also used in TCP flags
Confirms receipt of data; used in handshake and ongoing data transfer
Flag to gracefully terminate a TCP connection
Immediately terminates connection; used when error or rejection occurs
Instructs receiver to immediately push data to application; used for interactive traffic
Indicates urgent data that should be processed immediately
Maximum data payload TCP can send in one packet; typically 1460 bytes
Time for packet to travel from sender to receiver and back; crucial for timeout calculations
Time waited before retransmitting unacknowledged data
Sender's limit on unacknowledged data; part of TCP congestion control
Receiver's available buffer space; advertised to sender for flow control
IP header field limiting packet lifespan to prevent infinite routing loops
Largest packet size that can be sent over a network segment without fragmentation

visualTips.visualization: Imagine UDP as throwing postcards into a mailbox - you write, stamp, and forget (no confirmation). TCP is like a phone call - you dial (SYN), the other person answers (SYN-ACK), you confirm (ACK), then talk (data transfer), and finally say goodbye (FIN).

Picture a conveyor belt (data stream) with a sliding window frame. Only packets within the frame can be sent. As ACK arrives, the window slides forward, allowing new packets to be sent. The window size is determined by receiver's buffer capacity (RWND).

Visualize two people meeting: Person A extends hand (SYN), Person B shakes it and nods (SYN-ACK), Person A nods back (ACK). Now they can exchange information. If any step fails, no conversation happens.

Think of flow control as a narrow door (receiver's buffer) - too much data and it blocks. Congestion control is like traffic jam on a road (network) - too many packets and everything slows down. Both limit how fast sender can transmit.

Think of IP like a postal address: Country (Network ID) → City (Subnet) → Street → House number (Host ID). Routers are like post offices that read this address and forward packets to the correct destination.
Imagine sending numbered letters in order. Recipient acknowledges receiving letter #100 by saying 'Got #100, send #201.' This ensures ordered delivery and detects missing packets.

analogies.analogy: Like sending a text message or WhatsApp message without delivery confirmation. You send it and hope it reaches. No guarantee, no retry, no order - just fast delivery. Perfect for live streaming where missing a frame is better than waiting.

Like a formal phone call or video conference. You establish connection first, then talk, ensure the other person hears everything, can ask for repetition if missed (retransmission), and finally say goodbye to end properly.

Like a water hose: If you (sender) pump water too fast into a garden hose connected to a small bucket (receiver), the bucket overflows and water is wasted. Flow control is the bucket telling you 'slow down, I'm full!' - sender adjusts rate based on receiver's capacity.

Like traffic on a highway during peak hours. If too many cars (packets) enter, traffic slows down (congestion). TCP acts like traffic control: slows down when detecting congestion (packet loss, timeout), speeds up gradually when clear. Mechanisms like slow start, congestion avoidance.

Like sending an important document by courier. If you don't get a delivery confirmation (ACK) within expected time (RTO), you resend another copy. The receiver discards duplicates but acknowledges the first successful receipt.

Like a physical letter envelope: The address (IP header) tells postal service where to deliver, while the letter inside (TCP/UDP data payload) is the actual message. The envelope has sender address, recipient address, stamps (flags), etc.

If IP address is like a building's street address, port numbers are like apartment numbers within that building. Multiple applications can run on one computer (same IP), distinguished by unique port numbers (80 for HTTP, 443 for HTTPS, 53 for DNS).

Like a restaurant's table turnover: A restaurant with limited tables (receiver's buffer) can only serve X customers at a time. They tell incoming customers 'we can only seat X right now' (advertised window). Waiters (senders) can serve multiple dishes before needing confirmation that previous dishes were finished.
